name: openmind-glossary
description: Open Mind's deterministic glossary skill, run through the Open Mind skill bridge. Given 'define <term>' (or 'glossary <term>' / 'what is <term>'), resolve the term against the corpus's authoritative sources by exact token and return the VERBATIM definition with file:line provenance, plus a code usage profile when the term is also a code symbol. Unknown terms return insufficient_evidence — never a generated definition.

input.description: A term lookup request of the form 'define <term>', 'glossary <term>', or 'what is <term>'.

fields.name: question
fields.description: The request, e.g. 'define Ledger'. The term is resolved by exact token, not similarity.

output.description: Structured JSON with a status, the verbatim definition, source-grounded claims, and the tool calls used.

tools.name: glossary_lookup
tools.description: Exact-token lookup against Open Mind's deterministic glossary artifact (verbatim definition + file:line provenance).

tools.name: term_usage
tools.description: Grounded usage profile from Open Mind's structure map (definition sites, referencing files, modules). Only called after a successful lookup.

citationRequirement: Every claim must cite {file, line} inside the fixture corpus. The definition claim must cite the exact source line the definition was lifted from, and when answered the queried term must appear on a cited line.
unsupportedClaimPolicy: Definitions are verbatim extractions — never reworded, summarized, or generated. A term with no authoritative in-corpus source must return insufficient_evidence with zero claims.
failureBehavior: Unknown terms return status=insufficient_evidence with an empty claims array and an honest 'no authoritative definition found' answer. Never fabricate a definition or a citation.
validationRules: Output must match the required JSON schema.
Every citation must reference an existing corpus file and an in-range line number.
Each cited line must support its claim (shared keyword).
When status is answered, the queried term must appear on a cited line and the expected source file must be cited.
Forbidden/hallucinated claims from the test case must not appear in the output.
glossary_lookup must be called, and before term_usage when both are used.
